meta.name: PSAS Pre-Sleep Arousal Scale
meta.description: The PSAS is a 16-item self-report measure assessing the level of arousal experienced while attempting to fall asleep. It includes two subscales: Cognitive Arousal (racing thoughts, worry, and mental alertness) and Somatic Arousal (physical sensations such as heart racing, muscle tension, and other bodily symptoms).
meta.author: Nicassio et al. (1985)
meta.reference: Nicassio PM, Mendlowitz DR, Fussell JJ, Petras L. The phenomenology of the pre-sleep state: The development of the pre-sleep arousal scale. Behav Res Ther. 1985;23(3):263-271.
meta.instruction: Please describe the degree to which each statement is experienced as you attempt to fall asleep in your bedroom.

questions.text: Review or ponder events of the day
Worry about problems other than sleep
Thoughts keep running through your head
Being distracted by sounds, noises in the environment
Heart racing, pounding, or beating irregularly
A jittery, nervous feeling in your body
Shortness of breath or labored breathing
A tight, tense feeling in your muscles
Cold feeling in your hands, feet, or body
An upset stomach (stomach knot or nervous feeling)
Perspiration in palms or other parts of body
Dry feeling in mouth or throat

ranges.label: Low Pre-Sleep Arousal
ranges.description: Your overall pre-sleep arousal is low. You generally feel calm and relaxed when trying to fall asleep. This indicates that your sleep quality and patterns are in good condition, allowing you to get adequate rest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy sleep habits
Keep a consistent sleep schedule
Continue maintaining your good sleep habits
Avoid screen time and stimulating activities before bed
Create a comfortable sleep environment

ranges.label: Moderate Pre-Sleep Arousal
ranges.description: Your pre-sleep arousal is moderate. You experience some degree of mental or physical activation when trying to fall asleep. Mild sleep difficulties are quite common. Adjusting your sleep habits and environment can often bring improvement.
suggestions: Practice relaxation techniques before bed
Establish a calming bedtime routine
Avoid screens 30-60 minutes before sleep
Limit caffeine in the afternoon and evening
Establish fixed bedtime and wake-up times

ranges.label: High Pre-Sleep Arousal
ranges.description: Your pre-sleep arousal is high. You frequently experience significant mental and/or physical activation when trying to fall asleep. At this level, sleep problems may cause daytime fatigue and affect your concentration and emotional stability.
suggestions: Consider cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I)
Practice structured worry time earlier in the evening
Use body scan meditation
Consider consulting a sleep specialist
Consider consulting a doctor to rule out possible sleep disorders

ranges.label: Very High Pre-Sleep Arousal
ranges.description: Your pre-sleep arousal is very high. You experience intense mental and physical activation at bedtime. Your sleep difficulties are significant and may be considerably affecting your daytime functioning and overall health. Consulting a sleep specialist is recommended.
suggestions: Seek professional help from a sleep specialist or psychologist
CBT-I is the gold standard treatment for insomnia-related arousal
Discuss medication options with a healthcare provider
Address underlying anxiety or stress through professional therapy
Consult a sleep specialist for a comprehensive evaluation

cognitive.label: Low Cognitive Arousal
cognitive.description: Minimal mental activation before sleep. Your mind is generally calm. This indicates that your overall mental health is in good condition, with all areas of functioning working well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle and mental well-being
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Maintain regular exercise and social activities
Remember to seek support when facing difficulties

cognitive.label: Moderate Cognitive Arousal
cognitive.description: Some racing thoughts or worry before sleep. Mild psychological concerns are common in life. Focusing on self-care and healthy coping strategies can help maintain well-being.
suggestions: Focus on self-care with good routines and regular exercise
Learn simple relaxation and stress-relief techniques
Stay in communication with people you trust
If symptoms persist or worsen, consider consulting a professional
Maintain a positive outlook on life

cognitive.label: High Cognitive Arousal
cognitive.description: Frequent racing thoughts, worry, and mental alertness before sleep. At this level, psychological concerns may be affecting your daily life. It is recommended to take active steps and consider available support options.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional
Identify your specific sources of concern
Maintain a structured daily routine
Try relaxation and coping methods that suit you
Engage in meaningful activities and social interactions

cognitive.label: Very High Cognitive Arousal
cognitive.description: Intense mental activation with persistent racing thoughts that significantly disrupts sleep onset. Your mental health concerns are notable and may be impacting daily functioning. Speaking with a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Schedule a mental health professional assessment as soon as possible
Make sure you have trusted people around you for support
Follow the treatment plan recommended by your professional
Maintain basic daily self-care routines
If you feel overwhelmed, call a mental health helpline for immediate support

somatic.label: Low Somatic Arousal
somatic.description: Minimal physical symptoms before sleep. This indicates that you are currently experiencing minimal physical discomfort, with somatic symptoms within the normal range.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy lifestyle habits
Get regular check-ups to maintain physical health
Keep up moderate exercise and a balanced diet

somatic.label: Moderate Somatic Arousal
somatic.description: Some physical tension or bodily symptoms before sleep. Mild physical discomfort is common. Rest and healthy lifestyle habits can often help alleviate these symptoms.
suggestions: Pay attention to your body signals and rest when needed
Maintain regular exercise and healthy eating habits
Learn relaxation techniques to relieve physical tension
Ensure adequate sleep
If symptoms persist, consult a doctor to rule out physical conditions

somatic.label: High Somatic Arousal
somatic.description: Frequent physical symptoms such as muscle tension or heart racing before sleep. At this level, physical symptoms may cause discomfort and affect your daily activities and emotional state.
suggestions: Consider consulting a doctor for a physical examination
Understand the relationship between mind and body
Learn stress management and relaxation techniques
Maintain moderate exercise and social activities
Keep a symptom diary to help track changes

somatic.label: Very High Somatic Arousal
somatic.description: Intense physical arousal symptoms that significantly interfere with falling asleep. Your somatic symptom scores are elevated, and these discomforts may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Both medical and psychological evaluation is recommended.
suggestions: Consult a doctor for a comprehensive physical examination as soon as possible
Consider how physical and mental health may be interconnected
Develop a comprehensive management plan under professional guidance
Maintain basic daily self-care routines
Ensure adequate social support
